turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Alito has our opinion this morning in case 16-369, County of Los Angeles versus Mendez.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: This case involves a tragic shooting in Los Angeles County. Two deputies from the County Sheriff's Department were searching for a potentially armed and dangerous parolee and thought that he might be hiding in the one of the several structures in the backyard of his house. Angel Mendez and his wife lived in one of those structures, a one-room shack. At the time in question, the Mendezes were napping. The deputies did not have a warrant to search the shack and did not knock and identify themselves before entering. When they opened the door, they saw a man pointing what they thought was a rifle and they opened fire. Unfortunately, the man was Mr. Mendez, not the parolee. And he was not preparing to shoot the officers, he was moving a BB gun that he had kept in the shack. Mr. Mendez and his wife were shot and were severely injured. They sued for damages and argued that the deputies violated their rights under the Fourth Amendment because they should have obtained a warrant, should have knocked and identified themselves before entering, and used excessive force. They won a large judgment in the District Court. On appeal, the Ninth Circuit affirmed the judgment. The Court held that the deputies were entitled to qualified immunity for failing to knock and announce. But it held that they were properly found liable for using excessive force even though the Court found that opening fire was reasonable under our leading case on the excessive use of force, Graham versus Connor. The Court based its holding on a rule of its own creation, the so-called “Provocation Rule” which makes an otherwise reasonable use of force unreasonable if the need to use force was provoked by an earlier Fourth Amendment violation that was committed recklessly or intentionally. Under the Provocation Rule, the deputies were held liable for using excessive force because they did not have a warrant to search the shack. The Ninth Circuit also held in the alternative that the deputies were liable because the warrantless entry proximately caused the shooting. We hold that the Provocation Rule is unsound. Graham versus Connor established the test to be used in excessive force cases. If the use of force is reasonable under Graham, a plaintiff cannot prevail on an excessive force claim. The Ninth Circuit's alternative holding also cannot stand. The Court held that the warrantless entry in this case proximately caused the shooting, but the Court focused only on the risks associated with the failure to knock and announce. And since the deputies were held to have qualified immunity on that claim, it could not serve as the basis for a damages judgment. On remand, the Ninth Circuit must reexamine whether proximate cause permits the Mendezes to recover damages for their injuries based on the warrantless entry, not the failure to knock and announce. To clarify the scope of our decision, we hold that the Provocation Rule is unsound and that the determination of proximate cause must be revisited. We have not reexamined and expressed no view on the Ninth Circuit's application of Graham versus Connor. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Our decision is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
